The point `(x2=x1+width, y2=y1+height)` is intended to be the bottom right corner of the bounding box. Points within the bounding box must not be on that corner or the right/bottom edge.
Example: Let an image be of shape `1x1` (HxW). Let a bounding box cover the whole image with `x1=0, y1=0, height=1, width=1`. Then the bounding box is considered fully within the image according to the current condition for `is_fully_within_image()`, but not if the `<=` are replaced by `<`. A keypoint on `(1,1)` however would be considered both outside of the image and the bounding box.

Seems reasonable. But note that your image is only horizontally flipped 1/3 of the time, while often 1/2 is probably more sensible. Also, you get vertical flips 1/3 of the time, which can be a bit too strong or not needed at all (depends on what the model is supposed to be used for).
But if you really just want one of the three augmenters each time, then choosing `1.0` as the probability is the correct way of doing it.

I have a new proposal.

Perhaps the most self consistent proposal to date, because this syntax ~~is already used~~ will be reintroduced soon for use with constraints:

```python
y1, y2, x = variables('y1, y2, x')
model = [
    y1 == x**2,
    y2 == 2 * x,
]
```

This syntax is backwards compatible with existing code.

Unnamed model:
```python
x = Variable()
model = 2 * x
```

Named model:
```python
y, x = variables('y, x')
model = y == x**2
```

This is also nicely consistent with #30, allowing data to be provided by the name of the variable. In the last example, ```Fit``` would be called as:
```python
model = y == x**2
fit = Fit(model, x=xdata, y=ydata)
```
while in the case of an unnamed model, the ```data``` keyword should be used:
```python
model = 2 * x
fit = Fit(model, x=xdata, data=ydata)
```

This is because when you query Zendesk, the ticket has the status of open. The Ticket object that is created from the Zendesk response has the open status, and when you update it Zendesk happily applies the values in the posted JSON to the ticket, including the status value of `open`, reverting the change you just made in the UI. 

There is an easy workaround to this: you can explicitly set the ticket status to `None` before calling `update`. This will prevent POSTing the outdated status to Zendesk and everything should be fine. 

I can see though that this is a bit unexpected. The core of the issue is that Zenpy sends all attributes to the `update` endpoint even if they have not been modified. I could have a look at changing how serialization works to only send those attributes that have been modified. This would prevent bugs such as this and also greatly reduce the amount of data sent over the network.

I have to say that even as an experienced CMake user new to dlib, it took me a few minutes to figure out the unconventional organization (two top-level CMake projects in one repository and neither one in the repository root). I actually think that a better and simpler fix than 378431e would be to just have one top-level CMakeLists.txt that builds everything by default (dlib and examples) but has an option to disable examples (e.g. with -Ddlib_BUILD_EXAMPLES=OFF). If that's something your open to discussing, I can put together a separate pull request that shows what I'm talking about. I can also include some fixes to properly export the dlib::dlib target.

Did you not see the build instructions in the [README](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/README.txt) or the [web page](http://dlib.net/compile.html)?  I try to make it very easy.  For example, I want the dlib example [CMakeList.txt](http://dlib.net/examples/CMakeLists.txt.html) to be so simple even someone new to CMake will understand it immediately.

I'm certainly open to improving the build scripts, and adding things needed to support more CMake 3.0 features is cool (provided everything still works in 2.8.4).  However, I'm not sure I understand why you would want the top level CMake file to build the examples.  Wouldn't that make your external build scripts build them by default? I doubt people who are using ExternalProject would want that behavior.

Also, consider that I get questions [like this one](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35574967/how-to-use-dlib-in-projects) pretty much every day.  So while it's definitely important to make dlib convenient for expert users, I want to make sure it stays accessible to beginners (to the extent that that is possible).  I also want to encourage users to statically compile dlib into their applications.  So that should be the default recommendation in the documentation.  I say this because, in my experience, very few developers, even professionals, understand issues like C++'s [one definition rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/One_Definition_Rule) or that [mixing runtimes on windows](http://siomsystems.com/mixing-visual-studio-versions/) is bad.  Currently, the setup in the dlib example CMakeList.txt file avoids any possibility of these issues arising.  Even despite this, these kinds of user errors are so frequent (but only from non-cmake users) I've even put stuff inside the code to try and detect ODR violations or people doing bad things like writing `#include <dlib/all/source.cpp>` in their files (their compiler will drop them here https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/dlib/dlib_basic_cpp_build_tutorial.txt). 

But all that said, yes, please do submit PRs that improve the user experience for experts as well and thanks for this PR :)

I first made a clean `git worktree` and created an `sdist` (I un-tarred it to make sure) uploaded to TestPyPI to make sure the README looked good. Once I verified, I uploaded the `sdist` to PyPI.

To build and upload `manylinux` wheels, I just

```
./scripts/manylinux/script_x86_64.sh
./scripts/manylinux/script_i686.sh
twine upload wheelhouse/*
```

For the OS X / macOS wheels, I first download the Python.org versions ([e.g.][2]) of Python. Then I made a [Frankenstein][1] dynamic library for the Homebrew `gfortran`. Then I hacked `setup.py` (change will make it into the tree soon) so that the object files produced in `build_ext` were dual architecture ([see][3]) and so that the `.so` files were linked against my Frankenstein `libgfortran` rather than the one installed with the Homebrew `gcc` / `gfortran`.

[1]: https://github.com/matthew-brett/delocate/issues/23#issue-256356040

TL;DR; the encoding is wrong.

Long story: here is what we have on input:

```
2018-08-25 12:47:12,193 pyasn1: decoder called at scope Message.PDUs.ResponsePDU.VarBindList with state 0, working with up to 164 octets of substrate: 
00000: 30 12 06 0A 2B 06 01 02 01 02 02 01 10 02 41 04 
00016: FE FC E8 88 30 11 06 0A 2B 06 01 02 01 02 02 01 
00032: 11 01 41 03 30 C7 92 30 11 06 0A 2B 06 01 02 01 
00048: 02 02 01 11 02 41 03 34 83 83 30 10 06 0A 2B 06 
00064: 01 02 01 02 02 01 12 01 41 02 32 05 30 11 06 0A 
00080: 2B 06 01 02 01 02 02 01 12 02 41 03 2D 78 7A 30 
00096: 0F 06 0A 2B 06 01 02 01 02 02 01 13 01 41 01 00 
00112: 30 10 06 0A 2B 06 01 02 01 02 02 01 13 02 41 02 
00128: 17 B0 30 0F 06 0A 2B 06 01 02 01 02 02 01 14 01 
00144: 41 01 00 30 0F 06 0A 2B 06 01 02 01 02 02 01 14 
00160: 02 41 01 00
```

We are interested in this piece:

```
00000:                                     41 04 
00016: FE FC E8 88
```

Which looks like this as `bytes`:

```
>>> bytes([int(x, 16) for x in ['41', '04', 'FE', 'FC', 'E8', '88']])
b'A\x04\xfe\xfc\xe8\x88'
```

If we decode it as Counter32 it fails:

```
>>> from pysnmp.hlapi import *
>>> from pyasn1.codec.ber import encoder, decoder
>>> decoder.decode(b'A\x04\xfe\xfc\xe8\x88', Counter32())
...
pyasn1.type.error.ValueConstraintError: -16979832

```

But we can  decode it as signed Integer (if we fix the tag):

```
>>> decoder.decode(b'\x02\x04\xfe\xfc\xe8\x88', Integer())
(<Integer value object at 0x10cb21be0 tagSet <TagSet object at 0x1047560f0 tags 0:0:2> subtypeSpec <ConstraintsIntersection object at 0x10471ff98 consts <ValueRangeConstraint object at 0x10471ff60 consts -2147483648, 2147483647>> payload [-16979832]>, b'')
```

The problem is in the first payload octet e.g. `\xfe` - its most significant bit is 1 which in BER indicates negative sign. We can just add some more zeros (one octet) in front to get rid of the sign:

```
>>> decoder.decode(b'A\x05\x00\xfe\xfc\xe8\x88', Counter32())
(<Counter32 value object at 0x10cb242e8 tagSet <TagSet object at 0x1048df400 tags 64:0:1> subtypeSpec <ConstraintsIntersection object at 0x1048df438 consts <ValueRangeConstraint object at 0x1048df2b0 consts 0, 4294967295>> payload [4277987464]>, b'')
```

Although this analysis does not really solve your problem, does it? May be we should have a way to tell pysnmp to tolerate wrong encoding to some degree...? But it's not clear how to interpret it in this particular case, for instance. Should we reset the leading bit in `\xfe` or add some more zeros in front?

@dlitz In [this patch](http://paste.jaraco.com/i5bo7) against 83ab846c435b, I've put together a draft implementation of a new keyring implementation that does whole payload encryption, including the service and user names, and I would like your review.

This implementation intends to store the entire payload in a JSON document which contains a cipher info key with: salt, verification_value, initialization_vector, and iteration_count. The document also has a 'entries' key, which is the payload encrypted by the master key using the salt and initialization vector. The implementation strives not to store the master_password at all, but only store the master key in memory (and only when the payload is unlocked).

What I'm seeking from you in particular are answers to the following questions:

* Is it safe to use the same salt for the master key verification as well as the encrypted payload?
* Can you draft an implementation of the _PBKDF2_SHA256 and _SHA256 methods?
* Can the implementation be simpler?
* Does it seem generally sound?

I don't know why fitsio has been building successfully, but here is a hint. I did a test with python 2.7.2 and it appears that distutils.core.setup ignores keywords it does not know. It gives a warning like this 
```/opt/TWWfsw/python27/lib/python2.7/distutils/dist.py:267: UserWarning: Unknown distribution option: 'not_a_keyword_yet'

when I passed `not_a_keyword_yet=False` to it. 

For python 2.7.2, the build does *not* give this warning for `include_dirs` and also has the right paths in the compiler invocation! So apparently this functionality was undocumented and working for python 2.7.2, and now seems to not be working on my mac with python 2.7.10. I tried passing `include_dirs` in both places and got two copies of the include paths in the compiler invocation for 2.7.2. I don't have access to a linux box with 2.7.10 in order to test that system+build out. 

My feeling is that the script should follow the docs, so that as undocumented keywords break or don't break, the script still works. Thus `include_dirs` should be moved to the extension.

Sorry, I forgot to mention you need to use `pybedtools.set_bedtools_path("path/to/bedtools/dir")`.

The reason for this, and not for simply manipulating `os.environ["PATH"]`, is twofold: 1) multiple bedtools versions are supported, but different tools have been added over the years so we check ahead of time and monkeypatch a "NotImplementedError" when a tool is unavailable in an older bedtools version, and 2) at import time, `pybedtools.bedtool.BedTool` methods' docstrings are populated with BEDTools help strings. So there needs to be some module reloading (i.e. happens [here](https://github.com/daler/pybedtools/blob/master/pybedtools/paths.py#L17)) to remove the previously-monkeypatched `NotImplementedError` that was added when the first import couldn't find bedtools.

This:
```
>>> import win32api
ImportError: DLL load failed: The specified module could not be found.
```

This is in a virtualenv. It is trying to import one of the DLLs in pywin32_system32.

I don't know if the pywi32_postinstall script is even running, but I have my doubts -- the DLLs are *not* present in `sys.prefix`. In any case, I don't expect it to work. As far as I can tell, it installs to system32 or `sys.prefix` if that fails. I don't think `sys.prefix` is sufficient in the virtual environment -- it points to the root of the venv, which is not in `PATH` and does not contain python.exe (i.e. it's not a DLL search path).

`pypiwin32` seemed to work around this in the .pth file:
*pypiwin32.pth*:
```
import os;os.environ["PATH"]+=(';'+os.path.join(sitedir,"pypiwin32_system32"))
```

I've been browsing through the tests to get a grasp on what would be needed in the YAML structure. A few observations for tonight:

YAML should be parsed using OrderedDict instead of normal dictionaries if possible to avoid inconsistencies between test runs and to maintain consistency with the order in the data file.

Many tests include phrases that each map to distinct datetimes. For example, tests for special times would be more convenient to represent like this:
```yaml
special_times:
    - &special_times_2016-01-01
        source_time: 2016-01-01 00:00:00
        phrases:
            "noon": 2016-01-01 12:00:00
            "afternoon": 2016-01-01 13:00:00
            "lunch": 2016-01-01 12:00:00
            "morning": 2016-01-01 06:00:00
            "breakfast": 2016-01-01 08:00:00
            "dinner": 2016-01-01 19:00:00
            "evening": 2016-01-01 18:00:00
            "midnight": 2016-01-01 00:00:00
            "night": 2016-01-01 21:00:00
            "tonight": 2016-01-01 21:00:00
            "eod": 2016-01-01 17:00:00
    # We should get the same times with a source later in the day
    - 
        <<: *special_times_2016-01-01
        source_time: 2016-01-01 12:13:14
```

YAML &anchors also provide a convenient way to run the same set of phrases against a different source time. 

It might be useful to allow the configuration level here to specify other settings like DOWParseStyle. That could [and maybe should] be done in the test case but it would be nice to at least have a consistent way to represent the state of different settings in the data file so that the data file does not end up with multiple sets of results that behave differently for no obvious reason. If specified in the test fixture the configured calendar could be passed in as an argument to each test as would be needed for locales.

```yaml
example:
    -
        dow_parse_style: 1
        source_time: 2016-01-01 00:00:00
        phrases:
            ...
```

I've thought about this some more and it's not as simple as I initially thought.  

The issues are: first, that the output format dictates (in part) which sections of the .ini files are used, and; second, that the existing code is all designed for format to be available *before* the .ini is parsed and used.

Also, I assumed we were talking about web service.  CLI output format option can be passed with a wrapper script or alias trivially.

For CLI, the overhead in making the change vs a simple alias or wrapper script isn't worth it, IMO.  But I'll happily look at a PR to do it.  I would be more inclined to add a default format setting for web service outside personal.ini.

As for all possible options in every section, I'm not interested in doing that.  There are a ton of settings and over 100 sites.  It would be hugely redundant and a nightmare to maintain when they change.

What autogen=true does is to generate the images during the template render phase. Without autogen=true images are rendered on demand. In order to render images on demand we need sessions to make sure that nothing will be rendered unless it was requested by the template. In order words, the session stores a list of all the images that user could want to generate for the given session. Once the image has been generated, it is taken from cache.

autogen=true was added to be able to use image on html to be used on e-mails. It is applicable to any other case when the user who is going to watch the image doesn't have a session prior to watching the image.

I also use autogen=true when using Amazon S3, since there is no way to know when the user's browser will request the images. For this case I use a storage that works both on S3 and locally. Locally only creates symlinks. This allows {% image %} tag to work fast and take profit of S3 without the need of precaching images with a shell command. I will add this storage on the next update.

Ah, now I see where you're coming from. That exact use-case is something I had in mind, but I am not comfortable yet implementing it in the way you have. I am not convinced I want to rely on redis to keep session lifetimes, since it is baked into the session id already - I'd like a single source of truth. Furthermore, this makes time-to-live support change the behavior from a security point of view, which is not what I want (sessions should be "portable" across backends).

My own, less elegant but easier to understand idea for solving this was calling ``regenerate()`` if the remaining session lifetime is < 0.5 total lifetime or something similar. Basically, err on the side of "losing" a session instead of making security more complicated. This also has the advantage of working across backends, in case someone really is using filesystem-based sessions.

A bit of clarification on the motives and what it does: Installing  this via pip doesn't play nice with the included .txts, so this just provides a lot of ease of use for everyone. More importantly, if you're using this behind a webapp (ie me), and you want to easily distribute it across servers, then dealing with file location is a big problem because different server names. Also it inherently can't be cross platform. 

This solves that by putting the .txt files into made premade lists (it's easier to return, saves a little processing time if you're using this a lot on servers like me and has no serious downside) in separate files which are called if their name with no extension is put as the file location. The separate files are only imported on call by name so they don't degrade memory either, and including them by pip only adds a ~3kb to the package size.

OK, that's because Python 2.6 uses dict behind the scenes: ``` try: # pragma no cover from collections import OrderedDict except ImportError: # pragma no cover try: from ordereddict import OrderedDict except ImportError: OrderedDict = dict ``` The outcome of such tests is pure luck. A quick fix is to modify asserts to expect both `1` and `2` to be present in XML but not in any particular order. I'm sure there is a better way to write asserts about XML contents though.

I read a couple of wikipedia pages and RFCs last night. The division of responsibilities seems to be:

* STUN: ask `ifconfig` and a server "what is my external IP/port" (for UDP)
* ICE: try a bunch of connection hints until something works
* TURN: relay data through an external (public) server

The RFCs make it clear that this is all somewhat SIP- and VOIP- centric, so it all runs over UDP.

`wormhole.blocking.transit` is currently doing something morally equivalent to ICE (trying various host/port targets until one of them negotiates successfully), and the directly-connected/local-interface/ifconfig portion of STUN.

On one hand, it might be better to make wormhole's Transit code implement the actual STUN/ICE/TURN protocols, so we can take advantage of the existing work that's gone into them. Since we're not running SIP (and I don't think I want us too.. it sounds more complicated than it's worth), we either need a polite flow-controlled UDP-based stream transport (maybe uTP?), or we need a form of STUN/ICE/etc that works for TCP. I've heard of "STUNT" (STUN for TCP), but in my quick read of the specs it all seemed limited to UDP.

We also need to look at how ICE decides that it's successfully made a connection: wormhole.transit does a cryptographic handshake to confirm that we're talking to the correct endpoint (and not just some random server that happened to be listening on the same port), and ICE might not. The RFC I skimmed suggested that a MAC-based authentication might be used, but it's quite possible that the ICE security model is either glued deeply to TLS, or insufficiently strong for our tastes. WebRTC (see below) is glued to DTLS, which might be good enough for connection-establishment purposes, and we can always run our own encryption inside the DTLS tunnel.

On the other hand, if the protocols are too domain-specific, it might not be worth the bother. Or rather it might be worth more bother to achieve the same goals with something custom.

We should definitely look at what IPFS does, because they've got a similar problem space. I'm hoping for a solution that doesn't require too much C code. IPFS can be kinda casual about dragging in dependencies because it's written in Go, but for each C library we depend upon in Python, we lose some deployability to folks without compilers.

Also we should be looking towards the web: to allow browser-based implementations to interoperate with CLI tools, we may be limited to what a browser can do, which is WebSocket (now) and WebRTC (now/soonish). I haven't found any up-to-date pure-python WebSocket implementations, but it sounds feasible, and I think there are run-a-helper-in-a-subprocess ways to do it. Other languages have more support.

WebRTC is SIP-based (using STUN/ICE/etc), and a lot more complex (most of the libraries focus on the H.264 codec stuff, which we obviously don't care about (yet)), so I'm not as optimistic about finding easy libraries. *But*, WebRTC is supposed to have all the p2p stuff built-in. So in an ideal world, `pip install magic-wormhole` would give you a WebRTC-based transport directly to the other client.

A few pointers:

* WebSockets
 * https://github.com/liris/websocket-client (blocking)
 * https://github.com/tuxlinuxien/TwistedWebsocket
 * https://github.com/aaugustin/websockets (py3 asyncio)
 * https://github.com/Pithikos/python-websocket-server
 * https://github.com/gregmuellegger/django-websocket
* WebRTC
 * https://github.com/theintencity/py-webrtc
 * https://github.com/EricssonResearch/OpenWebRTC
* ICE
 * https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interactive_Connectivity_Establishment 
 * RFC5245 https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5245
* STUN
 * https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Session_Traversal_Utilities_for_NAT
 * RFC5389 https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5389
* TURN
 * https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traversal_Using_Relays_around_NAT 
 * RFC5766 https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5766
On the other hand, humanize is < 500 lines-of-code (not counting tests), so not really "big overhead".

```

Not sure I follow your logic here. Using a message queue with Socket.IO servers only two specific purposes:

- Scale horizontally to support large number of clients
- Be able to emit Socket.IO events from third party processes

Not sure if any of your reasons map to the two above, but these are the only two reasons it may make sense to use a message queue with Socket.IO.

> When I added the client_manager keyword to the socketio.Server class, it no longer functions

Did you monkey patch the standard library to be compatible with eventlet? The redis Python client requires that. The current version of python-socketio should warn you if you haven't done that.

> I am not entirely sure as to what happens to a message once I send it over the Redis message queue. 

The queue is normally not accessed directly by you, it is used internally by Socket.IO to coordinate efforts between the server(s) and any external processes that want to emit Socket.IO events. Both the server and the third party event emitters use classes in this project to interact with Socket.IO, there is no need to post directly to Redis.

> As a little bit of a side question, do I need to use Eventlet to run a Socket.IO server?

You don't have to. But I think you need to familiarize with the Socket.IO protocol and how it works, and then make a decision on what's the best web server to use. If you want to use WebSocket, then your options are eventlet or gevent, the standard Python server does not support that and will only use long-polling. My Socket.IO server does not run on Twisted at this time, so that is not currently an option.

Ok. I have a better implementation ready (which if it works, should be ok to go in main).

Please test the branch ``event_reset_once_more`` in my SoCo archive: https://github.com/KennethNielsen/SoCo.

With this I have added two changes.

 1. I have added a timeout option to ``unsubscribe`` on the subscriptions. When unsubscribing after a network change you just set a short timeout. The actual unsubscribing with the units will of course fail, but the internal bookkeeping of the subscriptions will be performed as if it had succeeded and we expect the units to clear that up themselves afterwards.
 2. I have added a ``https://github.com/KennethNielsen/SoCo`` module level function to events. It takes two arguments, the first is a waittime, in case you are resettings events on the same network and need to wait for the socket to be freed, the second is a boolean for whether to shut down the old event listener in a new thread. So for your usecase, you should just call it as: ``reset_event_listener(wait_after_shutdown=0, shutdown_in_thread=True)`` and it *should* do the job.

So to test, simply unsubscribe the old subscriptions with a (arbitrarily short) timeout and call the function to reset the listener. After this you should be ready to make new subscriptions again (and all if this with no blocking).

This is an issue with the documentation, we shouldn't be showing pagination parameters since the collections will paginate through all options. What `MaxKeys` does is set the number of responses to each individual `list_objects` request we make, but we will exhaust them all. To get a specific number, you can use `.limit`.

For the following example, say we have a bucket with the following objects:
```
foo
bar
baz
```

```python
import boto3

s3 = boto3.resource('s3')
bucket = s3.Bucket('foo')

len(list(bucket.objects.all()))
# 3

len(list(bucket.objects.filter(Prefix='ba')))
# 2

len(list(bucket.objects.filter(Prefix='ba').limit(1)))
# 1

len(list(bucket.objects.limit(2)))
# 2
```

You'll need to register handlers to certain events, specifically the `before-call` or `after-call` events. For delete objects, that would look like `s3.DeleteObjects.before-call`. The events handler of a client can be accessed with `client.meta.events`, the underlying client of a resource can be accessed with `resource.meta.client`. Your function will need to accept `**kwargs`, and you can pull information from there. For these events there is also a `context`, which is a dictionary that can be used to pass information through. Be careful not to modify any existing values, however.

Just speaking as a random user, at this point I don't think it's reasonable to expect `Instance.tags()` to change to return a dictionary, even with a major version bump.

*I think* the reason the tags come back as a list is because Boto 3 is now automatically structuring output to match the EC2 API. This is a [major design goal](https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/guide/new.html) of Boto 3. It makes things more manageable for the maintainers, who have to keep up with all of AWS's services, but it also makes it extremely unlikely that they will implement custom handling for this one method.

Having tags come back as a list of key-value dictionaries is un-Pythonic and annoying, but it's a minor issue with easy workarounds. I don't think any maintainer would break backwards compatibility to resolve that. The tradeoff doesn't make sense.

Hopefully a maintainer can step in here and clarify things so we don't hold out on false hope that this method will change. Or perhaps I am wrong and there is a practical way to fix this.

From the nginx docs:

> When searching for a virtual server by name, if name matches more than one of the specified variants, e.g. both wildcard name and regular expression match, the first matching variant will be chosen, in the following order of precedence:
>
> 1. exact name
> 2. longest wildcard name starting with an asterisk, e.g. “*.example.org”
> 3. longest wildcard name ending with an asterisk, e.g. “mail.*”
> 4. first matching regular expression (in order of appearance in a configuration file)

So the exact scenario you described shouldn't be able to take place.  I am sure there are similar situations that could take place with wildcard domains `*.example.com` taking over `*.app.example.com`, but I get the impression that the logic for detecting that might get pretty complicated. Also I would actually want to deploy while under maintenance mode. One reason for maintenance mode could be that your new version of your application has incompatible database structure so you want to execute the following steps:

1. Put application under maintenance mode
2. Take backup of your database (which is guaranteed to be complete as there won't be new requests)
3. Deploy new version
4. Run migrations
4a. (Make sure migrations actually ran correctly)
5. Unmaintenance mode

Obviously you can take the following steps as well

1. Deploy new version
1a. People will see weird errors and crashes just after deploy
2. Put application under maintenance mode (you could actually do this, right after starting the deploy which might prevent the issues in 1a)
3. Take backup
4. Run migrations
4a. (Make sure migrations actually ran correctly)
5. Unmaintenance mode

This will be the forced method with your proposal. It has the advantage of being less long under maintenance mode, but the disadvantage of having unexplainable problems on your site

@njsmith this is also pip’s threat model, because package installation requires the execution of arbitrary code from sdist `setup.py` files be allowed. That code indeed could overwrite things in your home directory like your settings, or add things to your path, or any number of things. That’s why explicitly privileging pypi (a know, trusted index) and requiring hash checking is a good step toward security. It allows centralized control and elimination of known security threats and identify verification of the packages you are downloading in a distributed fashion. What did the lockfile you downloaded say about the hash you should be getting? It doesn’t match what you’re getting from the index? In order for this mode of operation to fail you need to have failures at more than one of the local machine, index and network layer because you’re talking about having multiple corrupted packages in your application stack working in concert verifying hashes against a trusted index, and in many cases the hashes themselves came from yet another uninvolved source. So now you need to have at a minimum, all of the hash checking in both pip and pipenv somehow tampered with such that it generates hashes that are identical to the ones you are hoping for, but installs yet other malicious things?

I guess what I’m saying is, if your local machine is compromised there is nothing pip or pipenv is going to do to save you. But we can ensure that the package you’re downloading is the one you were looking for, from the place you were supposed to search for it, which can provide one element in the chain of security.

I’m likely missing something here, but amending the tree after it is produced sounds a little off to me. What happens if there are conflicting dependencies?

Say we have this (theoratical) depdency tree:

```
A==v1 <- B<v2
C==v1 <- B<v2
```

Initial `pipenv lock` produced a lock file of `A==v1`, `B==v1`, and `C==v1`. Now `A` has a new version `v2`, but it depends on `B>=v2`, that `C==v1` does not support. Under a normal lock sequence, Pipenv emits an error because it cannot resolve.

The above approach for `pipenv update A`, if I understand it correctly, would instead

1. Resolve the versions using the current strategy, getting the same result as before—`A==v1`, `B==v1`, and `C==v1`.
2. Calculate dependency for the updated A (`Av2`), getting `A==v2` and `B==v2`.
3. Overwrite results from 1. with the result of 2., getting `A==v2`, `B==v2`, `C==v1`. C is now broken.

I guess this problem is conceptually the same as Nick’s first question—how would the dependencies be calculated in step 2? To do it correctly, the resolver needs to consider dependencies from the existing tree. This is like `only-if-needed`, but instead of upgrade when needed, it needs to perform extra checks to ensure the upgrade does not break things. But this would mean we need to perform something like a three-way merge in the locking mechanism, and I’m not sure if this is possible with our current resolver’s implementation…

I changed the method to this:

```python
def get_unpatched_class(cls):
    """Protect against re-patching the distutils if reloaded

    Also ensures that no other distutils extension monkeypatched the distutils
    first.
    """
    print("MRO")
    print(inspect.getmro(cls))
    print("Bases")
    print(cls.__bases__)
    external_bases = (
        cls
        for cls in inspect.getmro(cls)
        if not cls.__module__.startswith('setuptools')
    )
    print("external_bases")
    print(external_bases)
    print("---")
    base = next(external_bases)
    if not base.__module__.startswith('distutils'):
        msg = "distutils has already been patched by %r" % cls
        raise AssertionError(msg)
    return base
```

`/usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.4_3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/`
So the tail chasing took some time but I finally have it working.

I see #5030 introduced this. As I understand this, after #5030 the platform-specific and `noarch` directory in effect get treated as separate channels with `noarch` always having a lower priority.
Thus adding `--no-channel-priority` shows the expected result:
```bash
$ conda --version
conda 4.3.17
```
```bash
$ conda create --dry-run -n tmp -c genomeinformatics amplikyzer2
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: .

Package plan for installation in environment /home/maba/miniconda3/envs/tmp:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    amplikyzer2:      1.1.5-py36_8       genomeinformatics
...
```
```bash
$ conda create --dry-run -n tmp -c genomeinformatics amplikyzer2 --no-channel-priority
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: .

Package plan for installation in environment /home/maba/miniconda3/envs/tmp:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    amplikyzer2:      1.2.0-0            genomeinformatics
...
```
I strongly feel this is undesirable behavior and that *inside a single channel*, the package version must have the highest priority. Only on a version number tie should the platform-dependent build be preferred.

I should note that the system had been intentionally configured to make the `pkgs` directory world writeable.  My thinking was that this would allow all users to install new packages but not over write the packages installed by others.  Ditto to the `conda-meta` directory, and the files in there `urls` and `urls.txt`.

> It seems that the update attempts to replace the DLL directory that is used by the python process which runs conda. However, Windows disallows deleting or renaming open files

conda doesn't replace files, it just moves them to a special directory that will be deleted next time you run conda and those file are not in use. That's what allows conda to update things like `python` or `requests` in the root environment.

I think the problem you're seeing is caused because you installed Anaconda/Miniconda for all users, and ran conda in a non-privileged terminal. Then conda can't do its job of moving files in use because it doesn't have the right permissions to do it.

So the solution would be to run conda as Administrator. But let's ping @msarahan to see if he has something else to say about this :-)

You're welcome. The funny thing is that the first things I tried was a `PS1='%}'` and `PS1='→'` 🙄 . The minimal working reproducer is the concatenation `PS1='%}→` 😆.
So the actual error is just
```
$ PS1=→
→source activate

EncodingError: A unicode encoding or decoding error has occurred.
Python 2 is the interpreter under which conda is running in your base environment.
Replacing your base environment with one having Python 3 may help resolve this issue.
If you still have a need for Python 2 environments, consider using 'conda create'
and 'conda activate'.  For example:

    $ conda create -n py2 python=2
    $ conda activate py2

Error details: UnicodeDecodeError('ascii', '\xe2\x86\x92', 0, 1, 'ordinal not in range(128)')

```
but that gets masked by another bug that happens during error reporting: https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6759.
My general advice would be to always use Miniconda3 and use Python 2  in a separate environment, i.e.,
```
conda create -n py2 python=2
```
or if you use `anaconda` then
```
conda create -n anaconda2 python=2 anaconda
```
. That way, `conda` itself will always use Python 3 and doesn't run into those Unicode decoding errors.
As a business rule, we don't switch an environment beyond minor versions without a user explicitly asking for it.  See https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.11/conda/core/solve.py#L272-L280

```python
import pandas as pd
from pgmpy.models import BayesianModel
from pgmpy.estimators import MaximumLikelihoodEstimator
model = BayesianModel([('Light?', 'Color'), ('Fruit', 'Color')])
data = pd.DataFrame(data={'Fruit': ['Apple', 'Apple', 'Apple', 'Banana', 'Banana'],
                         'Light?': [True,   True,   False,   False,    True],
                         'Color': ['red',   'green', 'black', 'black',  'yellow']})
mle = MaximumLikelihoodEstimator(model, data)
print(str(mle._estimate_cpd('Color')))
```

Hi Eelco,

You raise a good point about your near nullspace.  The default sets the
solver near nullspace parameter "B=ones((N,1))".

What is the near nullspace for your problem?  For just "curl-curl"
operators, this space is usually the space of all gradients.  But answering
this question is very important, and will guide your solver choices.

Regarding AMG for nonsymmetric problems, you can look at
https://github.com/pyamg/pyamg-examples/blob/master/Nonsymmetric/demo_recirc_flow.py
which highlights PyAMG options for nonsymmetric matrices.  This is
definitely still research, but these options have been fairly successful
for some nonsymmetric problems.

There's also a lot of other work out there on nonsymmetric AMG methods, as
you've already found :-)  There could even be some specialized solvers for
your problem type out there, but I don't know of any off the top of my head.

Cheers,

Jacob

BTW, when a YARN job goes from ACCEPTED to RUNNING to ACCEPTED again, it usually signifies that the job attempt failed, and YARN retried it.  This is why we usually recommend setting `--conf spark.yarn.maxAppAttempts=1`, just to avoid this confusion.

Then, once the job fails, you can take a look at all logs across the job nodes via:
`yarn logs -applicationId <your_appId>`

This will dump the logs to STDOUT, so I usually redirect to a file, as follows:
`yarn logs -applicationId <your_appId>  >yarn.log`

Using regex to validate email addresses is futile. The standard allows so many things that the regex would explode.

See this page for the full regex generated from the grammar (BNF) included in the RFC standard for email:

http://www.ex-parrot.com/pdw/Mail-RFC822-Address.html

And even then it doesn't support the full standard...

Best bet is to make sure it contains at least some character before the @ symbol, and some characters after the @ symbol. Anything else will just have someone angry because their "email address" doesn't validate.

For example, requiring that an email contain a `.` after the @ symbol means you won't validate for these top-level domains that are dotless:

https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7085#page-4

The tutorial looks awesome! A few comments:

* Is it just me or everybody experiences training loss 8x larger than validation loss? I understand it may be possible due to unstable weights from backward update after every batch. But, still it is not my usual expectation. (Not to say that I have not seen similar issues before and I am still slightly puzzled by the root cause.)

* The model class is linear, but the proposed network is a 3-layer overkill (which may be related to the larger training loss). If you want a fun problem, maybe consider a `xor` function class: https://medium.com/@jayeshbahire/the-xor-problem-in-neural-networks-50006411840b

* Is this `mxnet.gluon.loss.LogisticLoss` equivalent to `SigmoidBCELoss`? Since you are using metrics, it may be worthwhile exploring `mx.metric.F1`.

* There are some magic hyperparameters to be explained: a strong wd=0.01, an Xavier=2.34 initialization. As an elementary tutorial, I would try to simplify them unless they are part of the intended purposes.

**Hook**:
```python
from airflow.hooks.base_hook import BaseHook
from airflow.models import BaseOperator
from airflow.utils.decorators import apply_defaults


class TestOperator(BaseOperator):
    @apply_defaults
    def __init__(
            self,
            sql,
            source_conn_id=None,
            *args, **kwargs):
        super(TestOperator, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.sql = sql
        self.source_conn_id = source_conn_id

    def execute(self, context=None):
        records = self._get_db_hook(self.source_conn_id).get_records(self.sql)
        self.log.info('Fetched records from source')
        self.log.info('Records: {}'.format(records))

    @staticmethod
    def _get_db_hook(conn_id):
        return BaseHook.get_hook(conn_id=conn_id)
```

Brotli  archive header is less than 1 byte -> independently decompressible blocks are almost the same as  independent archives.
The coding phase takes not much time, the most of the time is used for finding better data decomposition. Trying to compress blocks of different size in parallel will not add much overhead (comparing to special solution that estimates output size without producing output).

So it looks that writing some wrapper, that tries to find the maximum input block that produces maximal feasible output, is the easiest solution.

If we consider brotli as a format (not implementation) there is a way to create implementation that will be more efficient for specific data type and output size requirements (e.g. it may omit some computations and use precalculated tables). But brotli is a general purpose data format. If you use some specific data format (SEED?) it likely will be more efficient.

No, using `jQuery.noConflict()` doesn't make the upgrade more efficient. I'd daresay, that it doesn't help at all :-) It would help writing new code with jQuery 3 and leaving the old code intact, if that were the upgrade strategy.

We use RequireJS and mixing of more than one jQuery versions with `jQuery.noConflict()` would work well. However, we use some jQuery plugins and libraries like Backbone, which also depend on jQuery. Introducing a module. Introducing a jQuery3 module would mean in turn introducing Backbone3 and other "*3" modules. jQuery 3 is also a part of the framework and our interface to our partners. It would cause a considerable effort and spread the jQuery upgrade over a long time. Instead of it, we will fix the framework to work with jQuery 3 at first, and then release it as a breaking change. Making a non-breaking upgrade with two jQuery versions may work only for simpler applications.

About `jQuery.noConflict()` - it is valuable, but for other purposes. We develop an embeddable framework with widgets showing information from an ECM system. We use `jQuery.noConflict()` not to affect the global jQuery, which the portal page may be using, where the widgets are integrated.

So for [IPV6](http://www.opus1.com/ipv6/whatdoesanaddresslooklike.html) we can write some helper functions to look at the `server` parameter and try and detect the pattern. As for testing this... that's pretty easy since we're just building a URL here.

Steps:
* make a method
* test the method
* call the method from the location you're using...

so something like (my syntax might be off but you'll get the idea):
```

    def test_ipv6_url(self):
         result = build_url('https', 'foo', '888', '/some/path')
         self.assert_equals(result, 'https://foo:888/some/path')

        result = build_url('foo', 'fe80::200:f8ff:fe21:67cf', '1234', '/some/new/path')
        self.assert_equals(result, 'foo://[fe80::200:f8ff:fe21:67cf]:1234/some/new/path')

       # and so on until we've covered the various ways to build a url string
```

As long as we assert that we're building our URL patterns properly the action of actually communicating using IPV6 is out of our scope. You may yet do something like...

```
   if is_ipv6(server):
      socket.inet_pton(socket.AF_INET6, server)
      url = build_ipv6_url(protocol, server, port, path)
  else:
      url = build_ipv4_url(protocol, server, port, path)
```

... or something else of that ilk. The basic idea is box off the url building so you can test it separately, box off the detection of ipv6 from the building of url so you can test the detection separately. So in this case we're going to end up with at least 3 unit tests: `test_is_ipv6`, `test_build_ipv6_url`, and `test_build_ipv4_url`. If we want to be *complete* we should also test `__GetServiceVersionDescription` with a set of mocks to allow the exercise of our `if` and `else` statements under test to prove our flow control lands in the right spots. That would be an *intermediate* testing technique but I could show you how to do that.

You have to compile the function but it is not costly - FAST_COMPILE makes
this basically invisible. I call this hundreds of times in my code (think
several times per layer, per input to the layer) and the overall compile
time doesn't have a difference at all. As far as I know, you *have* to
compile a function unless you want to track shapes in the .name or .tag
(which is what I had before - not pleasant).

For outputs_info the initial hidden is supposed to be a projection from the
last hidden state (see
https://github.com/dagbldr/dagbldr/blob/master/dagbldr/nodes/layers.py#L518)
which should be an input to this "layer". If this is the case you should
have all the shapes needed in the inputs (assuming user has to define
hidden dim). If you init with zeros (which can work, but is worse) you can
do tensor.zeros_like(some_hidden_tensor_slice) assuming you are passing the
whole hidden sequence in - otherwise you can play other tricks. I really
recommend requiring previous hiddens as input - you need this for
bidirectional model with attention anyways.

You can definitely scan without needing that shape directly - that kind of
defeats the point of scan. If you have an implementation online I can take
look and use the right variable names, etc. to describe what I mean.

Hi @PLNech 

How funny it is to come back to this ticket 3 years after... I now have more clue about this!

Please have a look [here](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/howto/custom-management-commands/#management-commands-and-locales). This is for Django 1.11 which is the version I currently use but from 1.7 up to 2.1: "By default, the BaseCommand.execute() method deactivates translations".

**My use case**
I have implemented a search index for a model that is used for [multi-table inheritance](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/topics/db/models/#multi-table-inheritance). By doing this, I can search for pages, news items and event items for example (from `Page`, `NewsItem` and `EventItem` models). Like that, I use the same Algolia index. One search rules all models.

Those three models have a `searchable_type` field that is respectively equal to 'Page', 'News' and 'Event' when I run the `algolia_reindex` command. But the language of my site is Dutch and when I update a model instance through the admin, 'Page' is translated to 'Pagina'.

This impacts the search and particularly the `searchable_type` facet since the possible values are clearly displayed and there is now a dichotomy:

![screen shot 2018-06-22 at 17 15 37](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8321628/41784463-0efc5a86-7640-11e8-8945-314a615aa270.png)

---

Based on the [Multilingual Search documentation page](https://www.algolia.com/doc/tutorials/full-text-search/multi-language-search/multilingual-search/), I come up with three options to address this issue.

**Option 1**
Add a `use_settings_language` flag to automatically use the settings language.

We can provide a given settings file to any `manage.py` command with the `settings` option. This could be quite painful for developers to maintain settings files for development, testing, staging and production environments though...

**Option 2**
Add a `language-code` option to specify which language code should be used.

**Option 3**
Add a `one-index-per-language` flag so all languages listed in the settings' `LANGUAGES` constant are used one by one.

**Option 4**
Add an attribute to the `AlgoliaSearchIndex` but this does not seem so clean to me.

**Common work for all options**

1. Compute the index name to create/clear/reindex. Indeed, if the `index_name` is initially equal to 'my_index', we would have 'my_index_nl' for Dutch.
2.  Probably slightly update what commands display to provide information about the language(s) that were used

---

Please feel free to give your feedback and ideas!!

If the evaluation points are monotonic (eg: created using meshgrid) the section:
```
minx = np.min(dx)
miny = np.min(dy)
maxx = np.max(dx) + 0.5
maxy = np.max(dy) + 0.5
```

Could be changed to something like:

```
xbounds = [dx[0, 0], dx[dx.shape[0] - 1, dx.shape[1] - 1]
ybounds = [dy[0, 0], dy[dy.shape[0] - 1, dy.shape[1] - 1]
minx = np.min(xbounds)
miny = np.min(ybounds)
maxx = np.max(xbounds) + 0.5
maxy = np.max(ybounds) + 0.5
```
This way it doesn't search the whole array.

> Another option could be to take more arguments in __init__ but default them all to None, and the same in the methods. Then it's up to the user to only pass them once in __init__ or in each method call. This would be most flexible in terms of usage, but overall I'm not sure flexibility is a win here. I think most users will only call very few methods (usually one or two) and passing a few parameters two times isn't bad, and doesn't offset the complexity / cost of offering mutliple ways to do things. But in the end it's a matter of taste.

An intermediate solution is to allow that flexibility only for ``nside`` - that is, ``order``/``scheme`` and ``frame`` should still be specified in ``__init__``, and we would allow array ``nside`` values to be passed during the method call.

Hi Marek,

yes, thats intentional since to our knowledge the bidirectional version of cuDNN RNN  (at least how it is exposed in MXNet) does not respect variable sequence lengths in batches, or features an argument like mx.sym.SequenceReverse. The same applies afaik to the BidirectionalCell in MXNet. 
We need to make sure that shorter sequences in a batch are correctly traversed in reverse order from the last non-pad symbol to the first symbol so that state concatenation of forward/reverse RNNs is correct.
However, we haven't really looked at that recently so if there are changes in cuDNN RNN that we could leverage, let us know :)

Re: factor input formats, I think we have some competing design goals:

- Specifying options and input files should be consistent with the rest of the codebase.
- Formats should be consistent between train and decode.
- Formats should be robust and easy to validate (avoid human error or at least fail fast).
- Formats should be as simple as possible given the above.

Here are a few ideas to consider:

If we like multiple files, what about folding factors into existing options?  Something like:
```
--source corpus.en corpus.en.factor2 corpus.en.factor3
--target corpus.de
--num-embed 512,64,64:512
```
If we add target factors, we don't need any additional args to extend this.

Paste being error-prone is a good point, especially if some tabs sneak into training files.  A user could get "pre-tokenized" data somewhere that isn't totally whitespace sanitized, try to build a system, and get confusing errors.  As reluctant as I am to suggest it, what about Moses-style factor delimiters?
```
An|O exam@@|B ple|I
```
The difference could be that we only scan for them if we're running in factored mode, so we don't crash on random characters when users don't want factored input.  (Factored mode could be defined as having multiple input files or a specific flag in training and based on model config at decode time).  This would give us a decode-time solution and we could support both multi-file input or word-level factor-delimited input for training (both ease-of-use and consistency).  As soon as we see a single token with the wrong number of factors, we throw an error and report the exact problem to the user.

Hi @stevebovy,

This is a great idea, but I don't think it's something that we'll be able to address quickly because it may touch a great many different areas of Certbot and not many people have been asking for this.

I hate to send people away to other ACME client projects because I don't want to make it sound like we don't care about particular use cases, but have you looked at some of the other ACME clients to see if one of them might already meet your needs?

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/list-of-client-implementations/2103

You can see that there are quite a few different client options, not just Certbot. Notably Certbot has tried to provide a very convenient experience for new users, including the installer functionality that helps to install the new certificate in a web server, and the renewer that helps to autorenew certificates when they're near expiry. However, some of the other clients are much lighter weight in terms of their functionality, size on disk, memory consumption, and dependencies, and also create fewer temporary and configuration files than Certbot does. So you might find that one of the other clients would be more suitable for your embedded application, including just because many of them have significantly fewer dependencies than Certbot. Many of them are rather reliable and easy-to-use.

Since other clients don't typically have installers to edit server configurations, you'd still need to figure out how to handle that part yourself if you used a different client.

Something that is very lacking in the documentation, also means Let's Encrypt is not fit for purpose for a minority who can't control the incoming traffic on 80 (likely China / certain ISPs, Corp firewalls)

I read through a multiple of posts around this, almost seems like the ability to listen on 80 is considered a security gain / trusting measure because it's a privileged port on a Nix box. 

Although seems let's encrypt don't recognise a use for certificates outside of web severs.. for example an email server etc, I might just want a quick boot up on port 7722 of a node app to handle the auth then shutdown as I don't run a web server.. but go figure. 

Also means if you use automation like Chef for example you need to configure self signed certificates to be able to boot your nginx up given the configuration will have SSL on it must have at least a valid certificate.

Another option though if you run SSL only is to have a different web server serve 80 so it can ensure the certificate is downloaded before nginx try's to boot up. (Annoying setup)

My chef deployments now run through the following:

1. Install a self signed cert.
2. Boot nginx (with self cert) 
3. Run cert
4. Symlink certs to my location 
5. Reload nginx

You mean the test that's already converted to Selenium?

The changes in #3412 make it easier to follow, so I'll describe [this version of it](https://github.com/mpacer/notebook/blob/1f549fcce40b7043f76ff4b90228e0fd5d57bde4/notebook/tests/selenium/test_dashboard_nav.py#L20)

It gets `authenticated_browser` from a py.test fixture defined in `conftest.py`. That deals with starting a notebook server in a separate process, starting a Selenium driver, and navigating it to the files list of the notebook server. The Selenium driver is `authenticated_browser` inside the test code.

The first loop clicks down into directories in the list, until it reaches one with no subdirectories. At each level, it waits for something with the HTML class `item_link` to be visible on the page. Then it makes a list of such elements and stores it in a dictionary by the URL. If there's only one item, we assume that's `..` (go up a level); otherwise we get the second item, click it, and assert that the new URL is what the link pointed to.

The second loop goes back up the levels, using browser history. Again, at each step it waits for something with class `item_link`, then builds a list of those items, and checks it against the list of items it found on the page on the way in. Then it calls `authenticated_browser.back()` to go back a step in history.

This may not seem like much of a test, but we recently made it navigate directories without reloading the page, using the history API in Javascript, and there were a few bugs in that.

I'm doing some cleanup in the demo notebooks. Along the way, I ported a simple plotly notebook from the jupyter-incubator/dashboards_server here to test the behavior with recent versions of plotly. While the plotly figure does not resize automatically to the grid cell, I find that it also cannot resize to the width of the plain-old notebook view without a complete redraw. Try the following:

1. Make your notebook browser window take up the full width of your screen.
2. Execute a cell that renders a plotly figure.
3. Make your browser window skinnier so that the notebook page area responsive design kicks in and becomes skinnier.
4. Note how the plotly plot is clipped now.
5. Re-execute the cell.
6. Note how the plotly figure now fits the smaller available width.

The same behavior occurs when in dashboard mode. The dimensions of the cell output are at time of cell execution dictate the size of the plotly plot using the default `autosize=True` setting for figures. Setting `autosize=False`, a figure width, and a figure height gives more control over the output regardless of user browser dimensions at render time.

The new plotting_demo.ipynb added to demos in PR #302 demonstrates the use of a fixed figure size. (https://github.com/parente/dashboards/blob/23756feca44d26379204f72149a0b89e36044747/etc/notebooks/plotting_demo.ipynb)

References:
    - S. Xie, R. Girshick, P. Dollar, Z. Tu and K. He. Aggregated Residual
        Transformations for Deep Neural Networks, 2016.
Links:
    - [Aggregated Residual Transformations for Deep Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1611.05431.pdf)
    - [CIFAR-10 Dataset](https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~kriz/cifar.html)
"""

from __future__ import division, print_function, absolute_import

import tflearn

# Residual blocks
# 32 layers: n=5, 56 layers: n=9, 110 layers: n=18
n = 5

# Data loading
from tflearn.datasets import cifar10
(X, Y), (testX, testY) = cifar10.load_data()
Y = tflearn.data_utils.to_categorical(Y, 10)
testY = tflearn.data_utils.to_categorical(testY, 10)

# Real-time data preprocessing
img_prep = tflearn.ImagePreprocessing()
img_prep.add_featurewise_zero_center(per_channel=True)

# Real-time data augmentation
img_aug = tflearn.ImageAugmentation()
img_aug.add_random_flip_leftright()
img_aug.add_random_crop([32, 32], padding=4)

net = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, 32, 32, 3],

                         data_augmentation=img_aug)
net = tflearn.conv_2d(net, 16, 3, regularizer='L2', weight_decay=0.0001)
net = tflearn.resnext_block(net, n, 16, 32)
net = tflearn.resnext_block(net, 1, 32, 32, downsample=True)
net = tflearn.resnext_block(net, n-1, 32, 32)
net = tflearn.resnext_block(net, 1, 64, 32, downsample=True)
net = tflearn.resnext_block(net, n-1, 64, 32)
net = tflearn.batch_normalization(net)
net = tflearn.activation(net, 'relu')
net = tflearn.global_avg_pool(net)
# Regression
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 10, activation='softmax')
opt = tflearn.Momentum(0.1, lr_decay=0.1, decay_step=32000, staircase=True)

# Training

                    max_checkpoints=10, tensorboard_verbose=0,
                    clip_gradients=0.)

model.fit(X, Y, n_epoch=200, validation_set=(testX, testY),
          snapshot_epoch=False, snapshot_step=500,
          show_metric=True, batch_size=128, shuffle=True,
run_id='resnext_cifar10')

When attempting to replicate this issue, I ran into some odd behavior. For each test, I kept everything the same except `peer_reviews`, which I tried different values for.

First, I tried `False`:

```python
course = canvas.get_course(COURSE_ID)
assignment_template = {
    'name': 'CanvasAPI Assignment',
    'peer_reviews': False
}
assignment = course.create_assignment(assignment_template)
print(assignment.peer_reviews)
```

@liblit: based on your report, I expected it would incorrectly set peer_reviews to `True`, but it correctly returned as `False`.

When I tried `True`, it still returned (incorrectly) as `False`. I then tried a few other values:

`peer_reviews` | Response | Expected?
-------------- | -------- | ---------
`True` | `False` | ❌
`False` | `False` | ✅
`'true'` | `True` | ✅
`'false'` | `False` | ✅
`1` | `True` | ✅
`0` | `False` | ✅

I was suspicious that boolean values were simply being ignored, so I created assignments that had `peer_reviews` set to `True` (and confirmed with an `assert`), then used `edit` to modify them:

```python
assignment_template = {
    'name': 'CanvasAPI Assignment',
    'peer_reviews': 'true'
}
assignment = course.create_assignment(assignment_template)

# ensure peer_reviews starts as True
assert assignment.peer_reviews is True

# edit assignment to set it to False
assignment.edit(assignment={'peer_reviews': False})

# ensure it's not just the local object that's been changed
confirm_assignment = course.get_assignment(assignment.id)
assert confirm_assignment.peer_reviews == assignment.peer_reviews

print(assignment.peer_reviews)
```

This correctly changed peer_reviews from `True` to `False`. So, I tried a few more things:

`peer_reviews` | Response | Expected?
-------------- | -------- | ---------
`True` | `False` | ❌
`False` | `False` | ✅
`'true'` | `True` | ✅
`'false'` | `False` | ✅
`1` | `True` | ✅
`0` | `False` | ✅

These results are identical to the creation results, which was very surprising to me. If booleans were being ignored/dropped, both `True` and `False should have returned `True`. However, in BOTH cases, they return `False`.

So... I'm not entirely certain what's going on, especially since this seems to be opposite to behavior that you reported. Do you have example code that replicates the behavior you reported?

I've done some tests, to keep leader lock till TTL, following requirements should met:
1. New setting `checks` for Consul in patroni.yml should be empty:
```
consul:
  host: localhost:8500
  checks: []
```
2. There should be no Consul Server on Patroni nodes. The preferred configuration is to run Consul Client on Patroni node, this client will own a leader lock without serfHealth, and this lock will survive if this Consul Client will be offline for a long time.

Alternative configuration (not tested) is to connect to Consul on other node (not localhost). But Consul prefers to run Client on every node.

Incorrect configuration: if you run one of Consul Servers on the same node as Patroni and Postgres, then lock will be lost in 5 seconds after this node will go offline.

Notice: Consul v1.0.0 and v.1.0.1 had a bug preventing creation a session without serfHealth (https://github.com/hashicorp/consul/issues/3732).

If you run Gunicorn on front of Aiohttp you can use `graceful-timeout` [1], it will give you the chance with a time window for dring the connections that are in progress.

Looks like - I didn't know- that if the `graceful-timeout` is enabled, the default signal used to stop the worker is the SIGTERM. So, to avoid the default kill behavior of that signal you must configure that signal to do nothing for your Aiohttp process. Once the graceful-timeout has been reached, and if and only if the worker didn't stop a SIGKILL to ensure the stop of the process will be sent. [2]

So, IMHO the soft restart can be reached without touching the current Aiohttp implementation. But true, that Aiohttp could implement a default stop for drying connections that are in progress.  

[1] http://docs.gunicorn.org/en/stable/settings.html#graceful-timeout
[2] https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/blob/master/gunicorn/arbiter.py#L373

GEKKO constants are objects with attributes name and value. In this case, you want the value:

```
from gekko import GEKKO 
m = GEKKO() 
myConst = m.Const(1) 
myVar = m.Var(ub=myConst.value) 
m.build_model()
```

If you find this tedious, I point you to the documentation:
> There is no functional difference between using a GEKKO Constant, a python variable or a magic number in the Equations. However, the Constant can be provided a name to make the .apm model more clear

I've started a thread on [StackExchange](http://crypto.stackexchange.com/questions/34740/parallel-file-encryption-and-authentication-for-large-files), discussing the security our file format. I think it would be better and also easier to use GCM on the blocks. This provides proper encryption and authenticaton of every block. It also eliminates the need for a final block which verifies all other blocks. One would only need to verify that the total amount of blocks is correct (the value itself is protected by GCM).

I've found the source of my problem.

I had a required field in solr schema (id).
When i change review state from folder_contents, a reindexObject(idxs=['review_state']) is fired.
c.solr's [index method](https://github.com/collective/collective.solr/blob/master/src/collective/solr/indexer.py#L223) check if there are all the required indexes in the query, and if not, it doesn't update the solr index.

reindexObject doesn't pass 'id' key, so the index is never updated.

The funny thing is that if i try to update a review state from the toolbar, that method will fire reindexObject() without indexes list, and this will produce an index update in solr because is correctly handled [here](https://github.com/collective/collective.solr/blob/master/src/collective/solr/indexer.py#L353)

btw my fix was to set _id_ key in solr schema as not required.

When Pants is installed using the setup script (https://pantsbuild.github.io/setup/pants) it does, unfortunately, have requirements that it neither checks for nor installs for you. In the end, this is because the setup script just uses virtualenv / pip in the end, and we have some platform-specific distribution dependencies like psutils.

There are a few paths to get things working here:

1. ~As-is:

     You'll need to meet the additional requirments described in the Pants [README](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/README.md), namely `libffi`, system headers, Python headers and a c compiler. For your examples above, these images demonstrate what is needed:

      **Ubuntu 16.04**
      
        $ cat Dockerfile.Ubuntu16.04 
        FROM ubuntu:16.04
        
        RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
          curl \
          gcc \
          libffi-dev \
          python2.7-dev
        
        WORKDIR /pants
        
        RUN curl -L -O https://pantsbuild.github.io/setup/pants && \
          chmod +x pants && \
          touch pants.ini && \
          ./pants -V

        ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/bash", "-i"]
        
        $ docker build -f Dockerfile.Ubuntu16.04 -t pants_issues_5642:xenial .
        $ docker run -it --rm pants_issues_5642:xenial ./pants -V
        1.5.0

      **Alpine 3.4**
      
          $ cat Dockerfile.Alpine.3.4 
          FROM alpine:3.4

          RUN apk update && apk add \
            bash \
            curl \
            gcc \
            libffi-dev \
            linux-headers \
            musl-dev \
            openssl-dev \
            python-dev

          WORKDIR /pants

          RUN curl -L -O https://pantsbuild.github.io/setup/pants && \
            chmod +x pants && \
            touch pants.ini && \
            sed -i -e "s|setuptools==5.4.1|setuptools==18.5|" pants && \
            ./pants -V

          ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/bash", "-i"]

          $ docker build -fDockerfile.Alpine.3.4 -tpants_issues_5642:alpine .
          $ docker run -it --rm pants_issues_5642:alpine ./pants -V
          1.5.0

2. Using a pre-built Pants pex.

    Although we don't currently release a pex, we intend to and this is tracked here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/4896. Once that issue is resolved, we'll likely update the setup script to just curl the appropriate pex if it exists.

I guess the term 'factor' is unfortunate in this context because it can refer to two distinct concepts:
- factor, the input of Alphalens (this is what I meant with 'factor' and what Alphalens means in its documentation)
- factor, one of the factors in a 'Multi-Factor Model' (this is what you meant with 'factor')

This explains why we have different meaning for X and Y in the equations ;)

So, after this long misunderstanding, I agree with you that it would be nice to have a  'Multi-Factor Model'  evaluation of the portfolio other than a simple one factor model, but I don't believe it's easy to do because we don't have the 'Multi-Factor Model' data. In the case of a single-factor model  (CAPM) Alphalens computes the market returns from Alphalens input factor averaging all the stocks returns. Given that the Alphalens input factor uses a big enough universe of stocks, the average returns is quite close to the actual market returns. I don't believe we can do the same for a  'Multi-Factor Model'. For example in the case of Fama–French three-factor model we don't have the SMB and HML data.

# first cell
from IPython.display import HTML

%%IML
a = I(6); * 6x6 identity matrix;
b = j(5,5,0); *5x5 matrix of 0's;

Short of waiting until we have added support for Django 1.8, my suggestion is to use the `UUIDField` as a supplementary key and allow Django to manage its own primary key behind the scenes. Something like:

```python
class MyModel(models.Model):
    uuid = models.UUIDField(default=uuid.uuid4, editable=False, unique=True)
```

If that doesn't work for you, you may be able to use [django-extensions](http://django-extensions.readthedocs.org/en/latest/field_extensions.html) to get the functionality you require. I have not worked with it personally, so I'm afraid I can't help you there.

Thanks for reporting this, John. This was an oversight on my end. The patch at http://dpdk.org/dev/patchwork/patch/40648/ does not have an associated series as it was processed before the upgrade to Patchwork 2.0 (which added series) support. As a result, attempting to download the patch with it's dependencies fails. Clearly we're missing a check to ensure a patch has a series before we attempt to build the dependencies.

There are two fixes necessary here. Firstly, a server-side fix to ensure a patch has a series. If this is not the case, we should default to download the patch without dependencies. Secondly, `git-pw` should handle HTTP 500 errors better than it does currently. I'm going to retarget this bug at the latter and open a new bug against getpatchwork/patchwork for the former.

Take a look at the `inverse_map` parameter to see the types of images that can be transformed (gray-scale, color, N-dimensional).

You have to handle the following cases:

- `inverse_map` is a callable and input is gray-scale.  Use currently implemented method.
- `inverse_map` is a callable and input is color.  Use currently implemented method, but remember to add back third dimension.
- `inverse_map` is a set of array coordinates of shape `(D, coord_0, coord_1, ..., coord_{D-1})`.  In this case, take the coordinates, slice off the first dimension, and take the maximum over the `coords`.

>There's no error, but the servos just don't move. Why could that be?

Did you set the compliancy to False before? By default, dynamixel motors are compliant and thus won't move. Something like, should do the trick:
```
m.compliant = False
```

>Also, what exactly does "sync_read" and "orientation": direct/indirect mean? And I'm guessing "offset" means the servos go to that position when the program starts?

_sync_read_ is a specific dynamixel instruction that allows to read the same register for a set of motors in one reading. It speedup the communication but it's not available for dynamixel protocol 1 except if you are using a USB2AX dongle.

_orientation_ sets the default orientation of your motor to clockwise or anticlockwise.

_offset_ is a software shift of the motor 0 position.

If `@hook` can be transitioned, so can `@action` as they are pretty much identical. There is no reason to pile it onto this PR though, as it seems to be a separate issue.

@johnsca Similarly, I'm not sure why `@action` needs to block on being able to mix `@when` decorators. Fair enough if fixing `@hook` is short term, as it will save some typing. But any approach to fixing `@hook` will also apply to `@setup` and `@action`.

The solution I used at first was some kind of a one shot brute force in the code itself to check my hypothesis. Something like this:

from docx import Document
from docx.oxml.shared import qn

my_document = Document('without_images.docx')

# code inserting some images

doc_element = my_document._part._element
docPrs = doc_element.findall('.//' + qn('wp:docPr'))
for docPr in docPrs:
    docPr.set('id',str(int(docPr.get('id'))+100000))

my_document.save('with_images.docx')

And the output file was finally ok without having to remove the images in the header. A quick test with opc-diag seemed to show that when adding images with Word later on, it keeps using the lower unused ids without worrying about these high value ones.
This solution is straightforward and simple and may be all that is needed (it was for me) but is of course thought to be done once only at the end of all the insertions (the pool of ids could be considered messy if that step was to be repeated multiple times).

Now however I'm working with a modified property next_id in the document.py file:

    @property
    def next_id(self):
        """
        The next available positive integer id value in this document. Gaps
        in id sequence are filled. The id attribute value is unique in the
        document, without regard to the element type it appears on.
        """
        id_str_lst = self._element.xpath('//@id')
        used_ids = [int(id_str) for id_str in id_str_lst if id_str.isdigit() and int(id_str)>100000]
        for n in range(100001, len(used_ids)+100002):
            if n not in used_ids:
                return n
				
The idea is that all python-docx generated ids from this property live in have their own space at above 100000. It seems to work but unfortunately I haven't had the time yet to test it thoroughly in different scenario.

Response in reverse order :-)

> (3) Another related thing to consider is formatting more than one
record.

Yes, I completely agree that this needs to be taken into account since it affects the output (the obvious case is MARCXML ``<collection>``-tag).

> (2) Formatting pure JSON representation of a record may not be always
enough when we need to show information from other records to generate
the wanted output format.

I understand the problem you are describing of including dynamic data in the formats. In my opinion the dynamic data should be computed prior to the formatting (or perhaps rather call it rendering). Hence, the input to the formatter is some representation that contains all the information needed to render the output. I.e. querying for 312 conference proceedings should be done in an intermediate step prior to rendering the output. In my opinion this step should not happen inside the formatting.

> (1) More nuance is needed concerning "support only Jinja based
formatting" and "removing XSLT" transformations.

Yes, it's perhaps a bit too aggressive formulation and perhaps we want to replace Jinja in the future, so keeping the concept of engines makes sense to me, as long as these engines are properly isolated from one another.

> E.g. one can keep "formatter" to server only JSON-based formatting use cases, and invent a new module, say "exporter" or "disseminator", that would take care of creating/serving various wanted formats out of native ones independently. 

I think I agree with this approach. Most of the cases you list I think is a transformation between different data models using existing defined transformations. Separating this out I think will likely make the code cleaner.

> In one way or another, one should be able to call some high-level API "to format this record in this format", which would sometimes spit out master format directly, sometimes spit out pre-cached format from "dissemination information store"

Yes, I do agree that we need high-level APIs that orchestrates rendering/formatting of records. My main issue with the current formatter is that its orchestration is not made up of small isolated modules that only does a very well defined job. E.g. caching and formatting is very intertwined, and there's all sorts of performance hacks. This makes maintenance error prone and very hard, you'll have a hard time moving e.g. caching to redis instead of bibfmt table.
